questions.question_text: How many types of licensee relationships does Alaska recognize?
distractors: Two types
Three types
Five types
questions.explanation: Alaska recognizes four licensee relationships: (1) licensee of the seller/landlord, (2) licensee of the buyer/tenant, (3) neutral licensee, and (4) designated representatives. Traditional dual agency was abolished in 2005.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants their agent to also help the seller in the same transaction. How should this be handled under Alaska law?
questions.correct_answer: Serve as a neutral licensee with written consent
distractors: Act as a dual agent with verbal consent from both
Refuse because dual agency is mandatory in Alaska
Withdraw from representing either party entirely
questions.explanation: Alaska abolished traditional dual agency in 2005. Instead, a licensee may serve as a neutral licensee — facilitating the transaction for both parties with written consent. The neutral licensee does not represent either party.

questions.question_text: In Alaska, what replaced traditional dual agency when it was abolished?
distractors: Designated agency only
Transaction brokerage
Limited representation
questions.explanation: Alaska abolished dual agency in 2005 and replaced it with the neutral licensee concept under AS 08.88.610. Unlike a dual agent, a neutral licensee does not represent either party but provides specific assistance to both with written consent.

questions.question_text: A brokerage has both a listing agent and a buyer's agent in the same transaction. What relationship structure can the broker use?
distractors: Dual agency with written consent from both
Both agents must become neutral licensees
One agent must withdraw from the transaction
questions.explanation: Under Alaska's designated representatives model, the broker designates individual licensees within the firm to represent each party separately. This avoids the need for a neutral licensee arrangement when the same brokerage serves both sides.

questions.question_text: Under Alaska law, a licensee who provides services to one party in a transaction represents which parties?
questions.correct_answer: Only that one party unless agreed otherwise
distractors: Both parties equally by default
The party who pays the commission
Neither party until a written agreement exists
questions.explanation: Alaska law provides that a licensee who provides real estate services to one party represents ONLY that party unless the parties to the transaction agree otherwise in writing. This is a fundamental principle of Alaska's agency law.

questions.question_text: A neutral licensee in Alaska learns that the seller is willing to accept a lower price. Can the neutral licensee share this with the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: No, unless the seller gives written permission
distractors: Yes, neutral licensees must share all information
Yes, if it would benefit the transaction
No, a neutral licensee cannot know this information
questions.explanation: A neutral licensee must not disclose confidential information of either party to the other without written permission. The seller's willingness to accept a lower price is confidential and cannot be shared without the seller's express written consent.

questions.question_text: What is the primary legal distinction between a neutral licensee and a traditional dual agent?
questions.correct_answer: A neutral licensee does not represent either party
distractors: A neutral licensee cannot disclose material facts
A neutral licensee earns no commission
A neutral licensee works without a written agreement
questions.explanation: The key distinction is that a neutral licensee does NOT represent either party — they facilitate the transaction. A traditional dual agent attempts to represent both parties, creating inherent conflicts. Neutral licensees still must disclose material facts and can earn compensation.

questions.question_text: A neutral licensee discovers significant foundation damage during a property showing. What must the neutral licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose this material fact to both parties
distractors: Disclose only to the buyer as the beneficiary
Keep it confidential from both parties
Report it only to the seller's broker
questions.explanation: While a neutral licensee must keep confidential information private, they are still obligated to disclose material facts known about the property to both parties. Foundation damage is a material fact affecting the property's value and condition.

questions.question_text: Both parties in an Alaska transaction consent verbally to a neutral licensee arrangement. Is this sufficient?
questions.correct_answer: No, both parties must consent in writing
distractors: Yes, verbal consent is acceptable in Alaska
Yes, if the licensee documents the conversation
Only the buyer needs to provide written consent
questions.explanation: Alaska requires both parties to consent in writing to the neutral licensee arrangement under AS 08.88.610. Verbal consent is not sufficient — the written agreement protects all parties and ensures informed consent.

questions.question_text: When must a licensee provide the written disclosure of their relationship to the parties?
questions.correct_answer: At the start of providing specific assistance
distractors: At the time of the closing only
Within 7 days of the first contact
After the purchase agreement is signed
questions.explanation: Written disclosure of the licensee relationship must be provided at the time the licensee begins to provide specific assistance to a party. Waiting until closing or after signing would be too late — the disclosure must come early so parties understand the relationship.

questions.question_text: During a transaction, a licensee's relationship with a party changes. What must the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Deliver a revised disclosure to all parties promptly
distractors: No action needed until the next transaction
File a notice with AREC within 30 days
Withdraw from the transaction immediately
questions.explanation: If circumstances change during a transaction that affect the licensee relationship, a revised disclosure must be delivered to all parties as soon as possible. This ensures all parties are always aware of the current relationship structure.

questions.question_text: What document must Alaska licensees provide to consumers explaining the different types of licensee relationships?
distractors: The Alaska Fair Housing brochure
The brokerage's internal policy manual
The Pearson VUE candidate handbook
questions.explanation: AREC publishes a consumer disclosure pamphlet that explains the different types of licensee relationships available in Alaska. Licensees must provide this pamphlet to consumers so they can make informed decisions about representation.

questions.question_text: A seller asks their listing agent what type of relationship the agent has with a prospective buyer. The listing agent has no relationship with the buyer. What should the agent disclose?
questions.correct_answer: That the agent represents only the seller
distractors: That the agent cannot discuss other relationships
That the buyer is automatically represented
That the agent is a neutral licensee by default
questions.explanation: The listing agent should disclose that they represent only the seller. Under Alaska law, a licensee who provides services to one party represents only that party. The buyer would need their own representation or the parties would need to agree to a different arrangement in writing.

questions.question_text: A neutral licensee must treat both parties in a transaction with which standard of care?
distractors: With full fiduciary duties to both
As though they represent neither party
With loyalty to the party who hired them
questions.explanation: A neutral licensee must treat both parties honestly and fairly. They do not owe fiduciary duties (like loyalty or full disclosure) to either party — their role is to facilitate the transaction while being honest, fair, and transparent.

questions.question_text: An Alaska licensee is designated to represent the buyer in a transaction where another licensee in the same firm represents the seller. What is this arrangement called?
distractors: Neutral licensee arrangement
Split agency
Limited dual agency
questions.explanation: When a broker designates individual licensees within the firm to represent each party separately, this is the designated representatives model. It allows the same brokerage to serve both sides without creating a neutral licensee situation.

questions.question_text: A licensee provides specific assistance to a buyer but never delivers a written relationship disclosure. What consequence may result?
questions.correct_answer: Disciplinary action by AREC for failing to disclose
distractors: The transaction is automatically voided
The buyer automatically becomes a neutral party
No consequence if the buyer signs a purchase agreement
questions.explanation: Failing to provide the required written relationship disclosure at the start of specific assistance violates Alaska law. AREC may take disciplinary action against the licensee, including fines, suspension, or other sanctions.

questions.question_text: A buyer contacts a listing agent at an open house. At what point must the listing agent provide a relationship disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: When the agent begins providing specific assistance
distractors: After the buyer makes a written offer
Only if the buyer requests representation
Within 10 business days of the open house
questions.explanation: The written relationship disclosure must be provided at the time the licensee begins to provide specific assistance. If the listing agent starts working specifically with the buyer beyond just hosting the open house, the disclosure is due at that point.
